refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by a tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safe operation). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a quantified design parameter that flows from this FR, not part of the FR itself. The refined FR remains solution-neutral by avoiding specification of network technology, protocol, or architecture.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input → tele-operator display output) under agreed network conditions. Confirm p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. Validate through safety case that this latency permits tele-operator to execute critical maneuvers (e.g., emergency stop, precision placement) without unacceptable risk.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator control inputs to the remote equipment with deterministic latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment sensor data and state feedback to the tele-operator with deterministic latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication availability under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation stages, with jitter control and priority queuing to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms.
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture (primary + failover) with automatic switchover logic and latency monitoring to ensure safe operation under variable network conditions.
DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms to prevent cross-channel interference.
DP4: Employ adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation with graceful degradation of non-critical telemetry while preserving command and safety feedback within latency bounds.

DP5: Implement edge-local autonomy with operator-in-the-loop validation, where time-critical control decisions are made locally and operator commands are validated asynchronously to decouple operator latency from safety-critical actuation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms to prevent cross-channel interference.

potential_sources: Shared network infrastructure may couple command and feedback channels if not explicitly prioritized; mitigation: enforce QoS tagging and separate virtual circuits.
Safety feedback channel may couple with telemetry feedback if not architecturally isolated; mitigation: define strict message types and routing rules per channel.
Operator cognitive load coupling: if feedback latency varies unpredictably, operator may over-correct; mitigation: maintain consistent feedback latency within channel budget.
Clock synchronization across distributed nodes could couple timing guarantees; mitigation: use deterministic time-sync protocol (e.g., PTP) or local deadline-driven scheduling.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves low coupling by isolating functional concerns (command intent, state feedback, safety assertions) into separate logical channels with independent latency budgets. This allows each channel to be optimized, monitored, and failed independently. Coupling risks are primarily at the infrastructure layer (network, clock) and operator interface layer (cognitive), both addressable through explicit design controls.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are characterized and stable enough to support p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed conditions; RISK: If network variability exceeds assumptions, latency budget may be violated. Mitigation: Establish network SLA baseline and continuous monitoring.

ASSUMPTION: Operator cognitive response time is compatible with 150 ms round-trip latency; RISK: If operator requires >150 ms to perceive and react, safety may be compromised. Mitigation: Conduct human-factors validation and define safe operational envelope.

ASSUMPTION: Safety-critical decisions can be decomposed into a separate channel with deterministic latency; RISK: If safety feedback depends on real-time sensor fusion or complex state estimation, latency may exceed budget. Mitigation: Pre-compute safety predicates and use conservative thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies define acceptable failure modes and operator authority boundaries; RISK: If policies are ambiguous or conflict with latency constraints, safe operation may be undefined. Mitigation: Explicit safety case linking policies to DP design.

ASSUMPTION: Implementation modules can enforce channel isolation without excessive overhead; RISK: Isolation mechanisms (e.g., separate threads, network queues) may introduce jitter or latency. Mitigation: Prototype and measure latency impact of isolation strategy.

ASSUMPTION: p95 latency is the appropriate metric; RISK: If worst-case (p99 or p100) latency is safety-critical, 150 ms p95 may be insufficient. Mitigation: Clarify acceptance criteria and define fallback behavior for outlier latencies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with three independent functional channels—command (operator intent), feedback (remote state), and safety (critical assertions)—each meeting a p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions, with explicit isolation to prevent cross-channel interference and defined fallback behavior for latency excursions. The design shall support operator-in-the-loop validation of safety-critical decisions and maintain consistency with worksite safety plan and operational policies.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the decomposition of teleoperation into three independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with isolated latency budgets, cross-channel interference prevention mechanisms, and channel-specific quality-of-service policies.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; routed through command channel with dedicated latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor feedback, and operational status; routed through feedback channel with independent latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety-critical signaling; safety channel operates with highest priority and strictest isolation to prevent interference from command/feedback channels.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical network transport; Teleoperation module manages channel multiplexing, prioritization, and isolation at the link layer.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. The DP directly describes the internal architecture of teleoperation (channel decomposition, latency budgeting, isolation). All three channels (command, feedback, safety) are logically contained within this module's responsibility. Minimal diffusion: only network transport and safety alert signaling cross the boundary, both via well-defined interfaces.

Poor fit. Safety Services would naturally own the safety channel logic, but the DP encompasses command and feedback channels as well. Placing the entire decomposition here violates separation of concerns and creates tight coupling between safety-specific logic and general teleoperation command/feedback handling. Significant diffusion of command and feedback channel concerns outside Safety Services.

Very poor fit. Vehicle is the consumer/executor of teleoperation, not the orchestrator. Placing channel decomposition and latency budgeting logic in Vehicle creates inverted dependencies and couples vehicle control logic to network communication concerns. High diffusion of teleoperation-specific concerns.

Moderate fit but overly broad. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could logically contain Teleoperation as a subsystem, assigning the DP to NGHE Solution itself (rather than to Teleoperation) lacks specificity and obscures architectural clarity. Better to assign to the most specific module (Teleoperation) that is a child of NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and most specific boundary for containing DP3. It owns the decomposition logic, channel isolation mechanisms, latency budget allocation, and cross-channel interference prevention. The three channels (command, feedback, safety) are internal architectural patterns within Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) represent the module's contract with the rest of the system, not internal diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If latency budgeting or isolation enforcement requires real-time kernel or network-layer support, some implementation details may diffuse into Vehicle or network infrastructure modules. Mitigation: Define clear latency and isolation contracts at the Teleoperation boundary; delegate enforcement to lower-level modules via well-defined APIs.

Risk: Safety channel logic may need tight coupling with Safety Services for compliance and validation. Mitigation: Establish a clear interface (SafetyAlert) between Teleoperation and Safety Services; keep safety policy decisions in Safety Services while Teleoperation handles safety channel transport and isolation.

Risk: Network Link module may need awareness of channel priorities and isolation requirements. Mitigation: Teleoperation exports channel metadata (priority, isolation class) to Network Link via a structured interface; Network Link respects these constraints without owning the decomposition logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module boundary is concrete and measurable. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ 150 ms' can be directly validated against the three independent channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets. Network conditions are specified as a constraint, enabling reproducible testing. Safety isolation mechanisms can be verified through fault injection and cross-channel interference testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR (end-to-end teleoperation communication with safe latency) maps clearly to the Teleoperation IM boundary. The three functional channels (command, feedback, safety) are explicitly owned by this module. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) define clear contract boundaries. Ownership is unambiguous: Teleoperation orchestrates decomposition; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert; Vehicle consumes Tasking/Telemetry.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR requirement. The decomposition into independent functional channels with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms is a sound design approach to achieve 'safe remote operation by a tele-operator' under latency constraints. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific protocols or hardware) and is sufficiently detailed to guide architectural decisions. No independence violations detected: the three channels are logically decoupled, and isolation mechanisms prevent cross-channel interference.

diffusion_risks: Latency enforcement diffusion: If real-time latency guarantees require kernel-level scheduling or network QoS enforcement, implementation details will diffuse into Vehicle OS and Network Link modules. Mitigation: Define latency contracts as SLAs at the Teleoperation boundary; delegate enforcement via well-defined APIs (e.g., 'reserve_latency_budget(channel_id, budget_ms)') rather than embedding scheduling logic in Teleoperation.

Safety channel coupling: Safety Services must validate safety-critical decisions, but SafetyAlert interface ownership is ambiguous. If Safety Services owns both policy and transport, safety channel logic diffuses across two modules. Mitigation: Establish clear separation—Teleoperation owns SafetyAlert transport and isolation; Safety Services owns safety policy validation and decision logic. Use a structured SafetyAlert schema to decouple concerns.

Network Link awareness: Network Link module must respect channel priorities and isolation constraints (e.g., safety channel preemption). If Network Link duplicates isolation logic, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: Teleoperation exports channel metadata (priority_level, isolation_class, max_latency_ms) to Network Link; Network Link applies these constraints without reimplementing decomposition logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 95 is justified. The Teleoperation module cleanly encapsulates the core DP logic: channel decomposition, latency budgeting, and isolation mechanisms. All three channels are internal architectural patterns. Diffusion score of 5 reflects only necessary cross-boundary signaling (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link), which are well-defined external interfaces, not internal leakage. The module boundary is tight and cohesive. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or duplicated logic across multiple modules.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the SafetyAlert interface contract: Define a structured schema (e.g., SafetyAlert = {alert_id, severity_level, channel_source, timestamp, isolation_guarantee}) to clarify ownership boundaries between Teleoperation (transport) and Safety Services (policy).

Document latency budget allocation strategy: Specify how the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is partitioned across command, feedback, and safety channels (e.g., command: 50 ms, feedback: 80 ms, safety: 20 ms). This allocation should be explicit in the DP or a derived design decision document.

Define Network Link interface for channel metadata: Create a formal API (e.g., 'register_channel(channel_id, priority, isolation_class, latency_budget)') that Teleoperation uses to communicate channel constraints to Network Link. This prevents Network Link from reimplementing isolation logic.

Add cross-channel interference test plan: Specify acceptance criteria for isolation verification (e.g., 'command channel latency spike shall not exceed 10 ms when feedback channel is saturated'). This ensures the DP's isolation mechanisms are verifiable.

Clarify Vehicle module's role: Document that Vehicle is a consumer of Tasking and producer of Telemetry, not an owner of teleoperation decomposition. This prevents accidental diffusion of channel logic into Vehicle control subsystems.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The Teleoperation module boundary is concrete, measurable, and well-allocated. However, the gate results expose three critical decomposition points that require next-level sub-FRs to manage diffusion risks and safety criticality: (1) SafetyAlert interface ownership ambiguity between Teleoperation and Safety Services demands explicit functional decomposition; (2) latency budget allocation across three channels (command, feedback, safety) is mentioned as a design decision but not yet formalized as a functional requirement; (3) channel isolation mechanisms and cross-channel interference prevention are core to the DP but lack explicit functional requirements to guide implementation. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-FR decomposition is necessary to prevent diffusion of safety-critical logic and ensure verifiable isolation guarantees.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-Teleoperation-1: Decompose command channel teleoperation communication with latency budget ≤ 50 ms (p95) and isolation from feedback and safety channels.
FR-Teleoperation-2: Decompose feedback channel teleoperation communication with latency budget ≤ 80 ms (p95) and isolation from command and safety channels.
FR-Teleoperation-3: Decompose safety channel teleoperation communication with latency budget ≤ 20 ms (p95), strict isolation from command and feedback channels, and preemption priority.

FR-Teleoperation-4: Enforce cross-channel isolation mechanisms to prevent latency spikes in one channel from affecting others (acceptance criterion: command latency spike ≤ 10 ms when feedback saturated).
FR-Teleoperation-5: Define SafetyAlert interface contract and ownership boundary between Teleoperation (transport, isolation) and Safety Services (policy validation, decision logic).
FR-Teleoperation-6: Establish Network Link interface for channel metadata (priority, isolation class, latency budget) to prevent reimplementation of isolation logic in Network Link module.

revise.why: The current DP3 is logically sound but underspecifies the allocation strategy and interface contracts. Before decomposing to sub-FRs, formalize: (1) explicit latency budget allocation across the three channels (command: 50 ms, feedback: 80 ms, safety: 20 ms) as a design decision; (2) SafetyAlert schema and ownership model (Teleoperation owns transport/isolation, Safety Services owns policy); (3) Network Link API for channel metadata registration. These revisions will clarify the DP and reduce ambiguity in sub-FR derivation. Recommended revision items from gate results should be incorporated into the DP documentation before proceeding.

assumptions: Latency budget allocation (command: 50 ms, feedback: 80 ms, safety: 20 ms) is feasible given network conditions (RTT ≤ 100 ms, jitter ≤ 20 ms) and Vehicle processing latency ≤ 30 ms.
Safety Services module exists and is responsible for safety policy validation; Teleoperation is responsible only for SafetyAlert transport and isolation.
Network Link module can enforce channel priorities and isolation constraints via metadata-driven scheduling (no reimplementation of decomposition logic required).
Vehicle module is a passive consumer of Tasking and producer of Telemetry; it does not own or implement teleoperation channel decomposition.
Cross-channel interference testing is feasible via fault injection and network emulation (e.g., saturation of feedback channel while monitoring command latency).
open_risks: SafetyAlert interface ownership remains ambiguous: if Safety Services owns both policy and transport, safety channel logic will diffuse across two modules. Mitigation: formalize ownership boundary in revised DP before sub-FR decomposition.

Latency enforcement diffusion: if real-time guarantees require kernel-level scheduling, implementation details may leak into Vehicle OS and Network Link. Mitigation: define latency contracts as SLAs at Teleoperation boundary; delegate enforcement via well-defined APIs.

Network Link awareness of isolation constraints: if Network Link duplicates isolation logic, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: export channel metadata from Teleoperation; Network Link applies constraints without reimplementing decomposition.

Cross-channel interference verification: acceptance criterion 'command latency spike ≤ 10 ms when feedback saturated' requires reproducible testing environment. Feasibility depends on network emulation fidelity.

Safety criticality of latency guarantees: if p95 ≤ 150 ms is insufficient for safety-critical operations, additional safety mechanisms (e.g., watchdog timers, fallback control modes) may be required. This should be validated with Safety Services module.
evidence_needed: Formalized latency budget allocation document with justification for command (50 ms), feedback (80 ms), and safety (20 ms) splits.
SafetyAlert interface schema and ownership model document clarifying Teleoperation (transport/isolation) vs. Safety Services (policy) responsibilities.
Network Link API specification for channel metadata registration (priority_level, isolation_class, max_latency_ms).
Cross-channel interference test plan with acceptance criteria (e.g., 'command latency spike ≤ 10 ms when feedback channel saturated at 95th percentile').
Network emulation environment specification (RTT, jitter, packet loss profiles) to enable reproducible latency testing.
Safety validation report confirming that p95 ≤ 150 ms latency is sufficient for safety-critical teleoperation scenarios (e.g., emergency stop, collision avoidance).
Vehicle module interface specification confirming it is a consumer of Tasking and producer of Telemetry, with no ownership of teleoperation decomposition logic.
